content.full_text:
State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 607 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K. STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

Technical Assistance Administration • • • Executive Legal • • • Enforcement • • • Conflict of Interest
(916) 322 5660                    322 5601              322 6641              322 6444

June 11, 1979

Charles S. Vose
Oliver, Stoever & Laskin
1000 Sunset Boulevard
Los Angeles, CA 90012

Dear Mr. Vose:

This is in response to your letter of March 27, 1979, and
our several telephone conversations in which you requested advice
on behalf of two members of the Covina City Council, Karl Jeager
and Frank Haven, concerning their obligations under the conflict
of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act of 1974. The
facts as I understand them from my conversations with you and
William Temple, Manager of the Covina Irrigating Company, are
as follows.

The City of Covina owns 40.3% of the stock of the Covina
Irrigating Company, which is a mutual water company but which
is nonetheless operated for profit. By virtue of the ownership
of this stock, the City can elect four of the nine directors of
the company. The City Council has decided to fill three of
these positions with Councilmembers. One of the three Councilmembers has an investment in the company worth more than $1,000.
The other two, Mr. Jeager and Mr. Haven, each own one share of
stock which they purchased in order to meet the requirement
that Directors be shareholders. Neither has any other investment in the company. Each Director receives $50 for every
meeting of the Board of Directors which he or she attends. As
both Mr. Jeager and Mr. Haven have attended more than five
meetings of the Board of Directors within the past twelve months,
each has received more than $250 for attending these meetings.

.
Charles S. Vose
June 11, 1979                                                    Page Two


     Section 87100 of the Political Reform Act of 1974 provides
that:1/

     No public official at any level of state or local
government shall make, participate in making or
in any way attempt to use his official position
to influence a governmental decision in which he
knows or has reason to know he has a financial
interest.

     Section 87103 of the Act provides that:

     An official has a financial interest in a decision
within the meaning of Section 87100 if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a
material financial effect, distinguishable from
its effect on the public generally, on:

          (c)  Any source of income . . . aggregating two
hundred fifty dollars ($250) or more in value received
by or promised to the public official within twelve
months prior to the time when the decision is made;
or

          (d)  Any business entity in which the public
official is a director . . .

     In the instant case, Mr. Jeager and Mr. Haven serve on the
Board of Directors of the Covina Irrigating Company because they
are members of the City Council; they have no private financial
interests in the company.  Through their representation, the City,
as an embodiment of the public, seeks to insure that the policies
of the water company are consistent with rather than inimical to
the interests of the public. The state constitution itself
recognizes the legitimate public policy purposes in the participation of public agencies in the operation of mutual water
companies. Thus, the general prohibition against stock ownership by public agencies contains an exception for the ownership
of "shares of the capital stock of any mutual water company or
corporation when such stock is so acquired or held for the purpose of furnishing a supply of water for public, municipal or
governmental purposes."  See Article 16, Section 17.  Such
objectives are not accomplished merely by ownership of stock


_____________
1/ All references are to the Government Code unless otherwise
specified.

Charles S. Vose
June 11, 1979                                                Page Three


but by the use of the powers of ownership of the company which
the stock represents. It is through their ability to control
appointments to the Board of Directors that stockholders direct
the policies and practices of any corporation. The most effective
way for the City of Covina to assure the quality and quantity of
its water supply is therefore to appoint as many of the directors
of the water company as it can. The directors thus appointed
who do not otherwise have any private financial interest in the
water company will be serving solely the public interest by
their membership on the Board.

     The purpose of the conflict of interest provisions of the
Act is to assure that public officials act in the public interest
instead of using their public office to aggrandize their private
financial interests. See Section 81001(b) and 81002(d). Here,
Mr. Jeager and Mr. Haven cannot be said to have any real private
interest in the water company -- they serve on its Board at the
direction of a public body in order to carry out its (public)
objectives -- and the payments they receive for doing so are
therefore compensation for services rendered to the public and
are analogous to government salaries. The Act does not contem-
plate a conflict of public interests and in an attempt to avoid
such a result, "income" as used in the Act specifically excludes
"[s]alary and reimbursement for expenses or per diem received
from a state, local, or federal government agency . . ."
Section 82030 (b)(2).

     In conclusion, the disqualification of Mr. Jeager and
Mr. Haven from certain Council duties because of their performance
of other public duties would be at all consistent with the
purposes of the Political Reform Act but it would foreclose one
effective means of public policy implementation. It would
therefore be contrary to the spirit of greater public accoun-
tability which underlies the Act and we will not construe the Act
to achieve such an anomalous result. Therefore, neither their
status as Board members nor the amount they receive for attending
the meetings of the Board serve as the basis for disqualification
from any of their decisions as Councilmembers. The actions
they take as Board members, however, are strongly analogous to
other decisions they make as Councilmembers. Therefore, they
should observe the policies of the Act when serving as Board
members as well as when serving as Councilmembers, and they
should not participate in decisions of the Board which could
foreseeably have a material financial effect any of their
truly private financial interests.

Charles S. Vose
June 11, 1979                                                    Page Four


     If you have any further questions, please do not hesitate
to call me.

                                        Sincerely,


                                        Sarah T. Cameron
                                        Counsel
                                        Conflicts of Interest Division

STC:mfa

NORMAN G. OLIVER
THOMAS W. STOEVER
RICHARD LASKIN
WILLIAM B. BARR
RONALD J. EIMBODEN
CHARLES S. VOSE

OLIVER, STOEVER & LASKIN
A PROFESSIONAL CORPORATION
1000 SUNSET BOULEVARD
LOS ANGELES CALIFORNIA 90012
TELEPHONE (213) 624-3071

VICTOR B. HANSEN
OF COUNSEL
TELEPHONE (213) 629-3171

March 27, 1979

Ms. Sarah Cameron
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. Box 807
Sacramento, California 95804

Re:     Conflict Issue - City of Covina

Dear Ms. Cameron:

     With respect to the matter that I have discussed with you on
two previous occasions, we have advised the three City Council
Members concerned that a conflict of interest does exist and they
will be unable to participate in the decision on the matter to be
presented.

     Since it is necessary to have a quorum vote on the matter, we
will utilize Government Code Section 37101 and choose, randomly,
one of the council members to vote on the matter.

     Considering the fact that we have determined a conflict exists,
it will not be necessary to have a formal decision from the FPPC.
Again, I would like to thank you for your help and assistance on
this matter.

                                        Very truly yours,

                                        Charles S. Vose
                                        Assistant City Attorney
                                        City of Covina

CSV:sp

sections.conclusion: In conclusion, the disqualification of Mr. Jeager and Mr. Haven from certain Council duties because of their performance of other public duties would not be at all consistent with the purposes of the Political Reform Act but it would foreclose one effective means of public policy implementation. It would therefore be contrary to the spirit of greater public accountability which underlies the Act and we will not construe the Act to achieve such an anomalous result. Therefore, neither their status as Board members nor the amounts they receive for attending the meetings of the Board serve as the basis for disqualification from any of their decisions as Councilmembers. The actions they take as Board members, however, are strongly analogous to other decisions they make as Councilmembers. Therefore, they should observe the policies of the Act when serving as Board members as well as when serving as Councilmembers, and they should not participate in decisions of the Board which could foreseeably have a material financial effect on any of their truly private financial interests.
sections.question_synthetic: Are City Council members Jeager and Haven subject to conflict of interest disqualification when serving as directors of the Covina Irrigating Company in which the city has a 40.3% ownership stake and they receive meeting attendance compensation exceeding $250 annually?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: Neither Jeager nor Haven are disqualified from City Council decisions based on their board service or compensation, as they serve in a purely public capacity without private financial interests in the company. However, they must still apply conflict of interest policies when making board decisions that could affect their private interests.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document contains both incoming request (Vose letter of March 27, 1979) and FPPC response (Cameron letter of June 11, 1979). Extracted fields reflect the FPPC response letter which is the primary advice letter. No explicit QUESTION/CONCLUSION headers but clear Q&C content present in the response.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Are City Council members Jeager and Haven subject to conflict of interest disqualification when serving as directors of the Covina Irrigating Company in which the city has a 40.3% ownership stake and they receive meeting attendance compensation exceeding $250 annually?

CONCLUSION: In conclusion, the disqualification of Mr. Jeager and Mr. Haven from certain Council duties because of their performance of other public duties would not be at all consistent with the purposes of the Political Reform Act but it would foreclose one effective means of public policy implementation. It would therefore be contrary to the spirit of greater public accountability which underlies the Act and we will not construe the Act to achieve such an anomalous result. Therefore, neither their status as Board members nor the amounts they receive for attending the meetings of the Board serve as the basis for disqualification from any of their decisions as Councilmembers. The actions they take as Board members, however, are strongly analogous to other decisions they make as Councilmembers. Therefore, they should observe the policies of the Act when serving as Board members as well as when serving as Councilmembers, and they should not participate in decisions of the Board which could foreseeably have a material financial effect on any of their truly private financial interests.

embedding.summary: FPPC advises that City Council members appointed to direct a municipal water company do not have disqualifying financial conflicts under the Political Reform Act, as their board service is a public function not a private financial interest, though they must still apply conflict policies to protect their private interests.
